assessment_prompt: Submit the culprit service, the failure mode, and the slow span inside the reference trace.
title: Search latency spikes after a catalog rollout
objective: Identify the slow span inside a single reference trace for a search regression.
prompt: The storefront search page is timing out under modest load. Follow the request from shop-web into edge-api and then into the downstream services. Your job is to identify the real slow span, not just the first service in the path.
hint_1: Start at the shop-web search trace, then expand into edge-api before deciding which downstream service owns the delay.
hint_2: The decisive clue sits on the search branch after the cache check. Find the slow child span, not the web entry span.
answer: catalog-api / broad Meilisearch query after a Redis cache miss

assessment_prompt: Submit the culprit service, the failure mode, and the slow span inside the reference trace.
title: Checkout slows while payment still succeeds
objective: Identify the repeated slow span inside a single checkout trace.
prompt: Checkout still completes, but shoppers are waiting far too long. Trace the request from shop-web into edge-api and compare the downstream branches before you decide where the delay really lives.
hint_1: The checkout root span only tells you where the request started. Move into edge-api first and compare catalog, inventory, and payment work.
hint_2: Ignore the healthy catalog and payment work. The inventory branch repeats very similar child work before checkout can finish.
answer: inventory-api / repeated stock lookup queries during reserve

inventory-api.query_text: select warehouse_id, quantity, reserved from stock_levels where sku = $1 and warehouse_id = $2

assessment_prompt: Submit the culprit service, the failure mode, and the slow span inside the reference trace.
title: Order history drags for active customers
objective: Identify the one slow history span inside a single account-history trace.
prompt: The account history page is slow for heavy shoppers. Follow the trace from shop-web into edge-api and isolate the specific downstream span that owns the delay.
hint_1: Move past the web entry span before choosing a culprit. The answer is in one downstream branch, not the page shell.
hint_2: Compare the order-history branch with the smaller product lookup that follows it. One branch is dominated by a single heavy query.
answer: orders-api / expensive sort in order-history query

orders-api.query_text: select order_ref, sku, total, status, created_at from orders where user_id = $1 order by total desc, created_at desc limit 20

assessment_prompt: From the mixed trace set, identify the healthy comparison trace and every regressed trace, then name the culprit.
title: Some search requests stay healthy while others regress
objective: Separate healthy and regressed search traces before naming the culprit service and issue.
prompt: Search is inconsistent after a catalog change. Some requests still look normal. Others are much slower. Compare the candidate traces carefully before you commit to the culprit.
hint_1: Start with the newest search traces and compare their downstream branch shapes, not just their root spans.
hint_2: The healthy comparison trace avoids the long catalog search branch after the cache check.
answer: catalog-api / broad Meilisearch query after a Redis cache miss

assessment_prompt: From the mixed trace set, identify the healthy comparison trace and every regressed trace, then name the culprit.
title: Only some checkout traces show the inventory slowdown
objective: Separate healthy and regressed checkout traces before naming the culprit service and issue.
prompt: Checkout performance is inconsistent. Some requests complete normally. Others stall in the application tier. Separate the trace set before you decide which service is responsible.
hint_1: Start at the checkout entry span, then compare how much work happens on the inventory branch in each candidate trace.
hint_2: The regressed traces show repeated inventory work. The healthy one collapses that work into a simpler branch.
answer: inventory-api / repeated stock lookup queries during reserve

inventory-api.query_text: select warehouse_id, quantity, reserved from stock_levels where sku = $1 and warehouse_id = $2

assessment_prompt: From the mixed trace set, identify the healthy comparison trace and every regressed trace, then name the culprit.
title: Order-history traces are mixed after a query-plan change
objective: Separate healthy and regressed history traces before naming the culprit service and issue.
prompt: History requests are inconsistent for active users. Some traces still look normal. Others now spend too long in one database-shaped span. Sort the trace set before you submit.
hint_1: Start with the history entry spans, then compare the downstream order-history branch in each trace.
hint_2: The regressed traces spend much longer in the history query span. The healthy trace returns faster and leaves more time for the follow-up product lookup.
answer: orders-api / expensive sort in order-history query

orders-api.query_text: select order_ref, sku, total, status, created_at from orders where user_id = $1 order by total desc, created_at desc limit 20

assessment_prompt: Pick one baseline trace from before the regression, one trace from after the regression, then name the culprit service and issue.
title: Search changed after a broader query rollout
objective: Compare before and after search traces to confirm what changed.
prompt: The search team suspects a new rollout changed query behavior. Compare one baseline trace with one regressed trace and identify the service and issue that changed between them.
hint_1: Use the before trace to set your baseline, then compare how the search branch behaves in the after trace.
hint_2: The after trace keeps the request open on the catalog search branch far longer than the baseline trace does.
answer: catalog-api / broader Meilisearch query after the change

assessment_prompt: Pick one baseline trace from before the regression, one trace from after the regression, then name the culprit service and issue.
title: Checkout changed after an inventory reservation deploy
objective: Compare before and after checkout traces to confirm what changed.
prompt: A reservation deploy may have changed checkout behavior. Compare the baseline and regressed traces and identify the service and issue that changed across the two windows.
hint_1: Use the baseline checkout trace to see the healthy shape first, then compare how the inventory branch expands in the after trace.
hint_2: The after trace repeats inventory work several times before payment starts. The baseline trace does not.
answer: inventory-api / repeated stock lookup queries after the change

inventory-api.query_text: select warehouse_id, quantity, reserved from stock_levels where sku = $1 and warehouse_id = $2

assessment_prompt: Pick one baseline trace from before the regression, one trace from after the regression, then name the culprit service and issue.
title: Order history changed after a sort tweak
objective: Compare before and after history traces to confirm what changed.
prompt: The history team suspects a sort change degraded performance. Compare one before trace with one after trace and identify the service and issue that changed.
hint_1: Use the before trace to learn the healthy timing pattern before you inspect the after trace.
hint_2: The after trace spends much longer in the order-history query span. That is the change you need to explain.
answer: orders-api / expensive sort after the query change

orders-api.query_text: select order_ref, sku, total, status, created_at from orders where user_id = $1 order by total desc, created_at desc limit 20

assessment_prompt: Submit the culprit service, the failure mode, the culprit span, and the supporting attribute that proves it.
title: Search slowdown needs span-level proof
objective: Use a supporting span attribute to prove the search diagnosis.
prompt: You already know how to find the slow span. This time you must also prove the diagnosis with a supporting attribute on that span.
hint_1: Find the slow span first. Only then decide which attribute on that span proves what changed.
hint_2: The search branch carries a lab-specific query label you can use as evidence once you have the right span.
answer: catalog-api / catalog.search.meilisearch proven by its span attribute

assessment_prompt: Submit the culprit service, the failure mode, the culprit span, and the supporting attribute that proves it.
title: History slowdown needs span-level proof
objective: Use a supporting span attribute to prove the history diagnosis.
prompt: You already know how to find the slow span. This time the coach expects proof: identify the culprit span and the attribute that anchors the diagnosis.
hint_1: Do not start with attributes. Find the slow history span first, then inspect its tags.
hint_2: The right span carries a lab query label that names the heavy history query.
answer: orders-api / orders.history.expensive_sort proven by its span attribute

orders-api.query_text: select order_ref, sku, total, status, created_at from orders where user_id = $1 order by total desc, created_at desc limit 20

assessment_prompt: Submit the culprit service, the failure mode, the culprit span, and the supporting attribute that proves it.
title: Checkout failures need span-level proof
objective: Use a supporting span attribute to prove the payment-path diagnosis.
prompt: Checkout now fails in the payment path. You must identify the culprit span and the attribute that proves the database behavior behind the failure.
hint_1: Ignore successful catalog and inventory work. Find the slow failing span on the payment branch first.
hint_2: The payment span carries a lab-specific failure attribute that tells you what kind of wait happened.
answer: payments-api / lock wait timeout proven by the failure attribute

assessment_prompt: From the intermittent candidate set, select every failing trace, then name the culprit service and issue.
title: Search only regresses on some requests
objective: Identify every regressed trace in an intermittent search failure set.
prompt: Search only slows down on some requests after the latest catalog update. Inspect the mixed trace set and select every regressed request before naming the culprit.
hint_1: Do not assume every search trace is bad. Compare the candidate traces and decide which ones actually show the regression.
hint_2: The regressed traces keep the catalog search branch open much longer than the healthy traces do.
answer: catalog-api / intermittent broad search query regression

assessment_prompt: From the intermittent candidate set, select every failing trace, then name the culprit service and issue.
title: Checkout only fails on some payment attempts
objective: Identify every failing trace in an intermittent payment failure set.
prompt: Checkout does not fail every time. Inspect the mixed candidate set and select every failing request before you name the service and failure mode.
hint_1: Start by separating the successful checkout traces from the ones that fail on the payment branch.
hint_2: Only the failing traces show the slow payment span ending in the lock-wait behavior.

assessment_prompt: From the intermittent candidate set, select every failing trace, then name the culprit service and issue.
title: Order history only regresses on some requests
objective: Identify every regressed trace in an intermittent order-history slowdown set.
prompt: History latency is inconsistent after a query tuning change. Some traces still look healthy. Others clearly regress. Select every regressed trace before naming the culprit.
hint_1: Do not grade by route alone. Compare the candidate traces and decide which ones actually show the long history query span.
hint_2: Only the regressed traces spend heavy time in the history query span before the featured-product lookup begins.

orders-api.query_text: select order_ref, sku, total, status, created_at from orders where user_id = $1 order by total desc, created_at desc limit 20
